coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device for field analysis of soluble copper in river water using carbon quantum dots as luminophore
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yuan
Wei, Min
Liu, Rui
Wang, Honghai
Li, Huijuan
Kang, Qi
Shen, Dazhong
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive detection of copper is valuable for environment and human healthy. Herein, we reported a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device for field analysis of soluble copper in river water samples, which is based on the fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ to N-doped carbon quantum dots. The fluorescent images in the sample and reference areas in a twin cuvette were captured by the camera in a smartphone. The non-linearity response of the image brightness to the exposure was corrected by (B−B 0)/(255 −B), where B and B 0 are the brightness with and without luminophore, respectively. It was shown that corrected brightness ratio is insensitive the variation in light source intensity, ambient temperature, shooting parameters and focus of the camera, which is helpful to improve the stability and reliability of the cheap portable detection device. By using a balanced calibration method, the accuracy for concentration estimation is improved further. Under optimized conditions, the proposed method was applied to detect Cu2+ in the range from 1 to 200 nM with the detection limit of 0.43 nM. This simple fluorescence device offers the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone-based
Double channels
Ratiometric fluorescence
Field analysis
Brightness correction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Field analysis of Cu2+ in river water samples
Performance of the smartphone based fluorescence device
Influence of the shooting parameters on the performance of the smartphone based fluorescence device
Influence of the focus of the camera on the response
Influence of excitation source intensity drift on the responses
Influence of ambient temperature on brightness ratio
Condition optimization for Cu2+ ion determination
Application in field analysis of Cu2+ in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the smartphone-based double-channel ratiometric fluorescence detection experimental setup for field analysis. (1) black box, (2) smartphone, (3) macro convergent lens, (4) long pass filter, (5) double-channel twin cuvette with its frame, (6) reflection film, (7) LED flashlight, (8) power switch, (9) portable lithium battery source.
Comparison of the calibration curves using averaged brightness (a) and corrected brightness parameter (b) as the signal indictors. ISO = 100, exposure time t = 2 s.
Influence of pH on fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in solutions with and without Cu2+. Experimental conditions: [N-CQDs]= 70 μg/mL, ISO= 100, exposure time t = 3 s.
Influence of concentration of N-CQDs on fluorescence intensity of the solutions with and without Cu2+. Experimental conditions: pH= 6.0, ISO= 100, exposure time t = 3 s.

The images of the double-channel cuvette (A) at different Cu2+ concentration and the calibration curves for Cu2+ determination in smartphone-based fluorescence method (B). Exposure time t = 3 s, ISO = 100. The reference contained 30 nM Cu2+.

The selectivity of the proposed fluorescence method. The concentration of ions in order: 1 mM CO3 2–, SO4 2–, Cl–, NO3 -, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, 0.2 μM Cr3+, Pb2+, Mn2+ Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe3+. Experimental conditions: [N-CQDs]= 70 μg/mL, pH= 6.0, ISO= 100, exposure time t = 3 s.
Comparison of analytical performance of some methods for Cu(II) detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: BMTTE: bis [o-(N-methylidenamino-2-thiol-1,3,4-thiadiazole-5-yl) phenoxy] ethane; BPEI: branched poly-(ethylenimine): CC: carbon cloth; HxTiS2 -PANI: porous HxTiS2 nanosheet-polyaniline; PEG: polyethylene glycol; rGO: reduced graphene oxide; TSPP: meso-tetra(4- sulfonatophenyl)- porphyrin.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CL: chemiluminescence; EC: electrochemistry; ECL, electrochemiluminescence; HR-ICP-MS: highresolution inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry, PEC: photoelectrochemical.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison the concentration of Cu(II) in water samples determined by ICP-AES and N-CQDs fluorescence quench methods measured by the developed smartphone-based device and lab spectrofluorimeter.

row.entry: *Data were obtained in field analysis. Sampling date: 2018/08/16.
The map of the sample sites in field analysis.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device for field analysis of soluble copper in river water using carbon quantum dots as luminophore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Clean Production of Fine Chemicals, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Clean Production of Fine Chemicals, Shandong Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive detection of copper is valuable for environment and human healthy. Herein, we reported a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device for field analysis of soluble copper in river water samples, which is based on the fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ to N-doped carbon quantum dots. The fluorescent images in the sample and reference areas in a twin cuvette were captured by the camera in a smartphone. The non-linearity response of the image brightness to the exposure was corrected by (B−B 0)/(255 −B), where B and B 0 are the brightness with and without luminophore, respectively. It was shown that corrected brightness ratio is insensitive the variation in light source intensity, ambient temperature, shooting parameters and focus of the camera, which is helpful to improve the stability and reliability of the cheap portable detection device. By using a balanced calibration method, the accuracy for concentration estimation is improved further. Under optimized conditions, the proposed method was applied to detect Cu2+ in the range from 1 to 200 nM with the detection limit of 0.43 nM. This simple fluorescence device offers the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an essential element of a balanced human diet, performing several functions in vivo including the catalysis of enzymatic reactions and formation of hemoglobin red blood cells [1]. For many years, copper has found fundamental applications in metallurgy and electrical industry due to its ductility and excellent electrical conductivity. The negative effects of copper from the accompanied effluent discharge on the surrounding ecosystem, including the ecotoxicological impact on soil organisms, plants and aquatic organisms, and the transference in food chains to cause the secondary poisoning effects on animals and humans, draw increasing attention [2]. Hence, the quantitative determination of copper in environmental samples is also important. Various techniques including atomic spectrometry [1,3,4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], fluorescent and colorimetric [6–12], optode [13], chemiluminescence [14] electrochemical sensor [15–18], have been developing to meet the increasing requests in sensitivity, selectivity, speed, accuracy and practicability. But most of the detection methods need analytical instruments, which are usually bulky, expensive and power-wasting, being disadvantageous in the applications for portable field analysis.

With the rapid improvement in hardware and software, smartphones are analogous to mini computers with internal memory, operating systems and high quality cameras, thereby providing opportunities to advance analytical sensing systems and their applications as reported in the literatures [19–22]. Typical examples include the optical-sensing platforms for colorimetry [23–26], fluoremetry [27–29], resonance light scattering [30], chemluminescence [31], electrochemiluminescence [32,33], as well as data analysis and control interfaces [34–36]. Obviously, a smartphone-based analytical device offers the advantages in portability, low power consumption, cheapness, and convenience in location and data exchange for field analysis [37–39].

The fluorescence-based detection methods show the great application potential in revealing trace analytes because of its high sensitivity and simplicity. Hence, smartphone-based fluorescent detection devices are rapidly developed [40,41]. Usually, the fluorescent image of the luminescent area is captured by the internal built-in camera. The image brightness is used as the analytical signal. To obtain a reliable fluorescent signal, an excitation light source with high intensity and stability is needed. Based on the consideration for field analysis, simple light sources like laser pointer or LED flashlight even ambient light are employed [27,30,37,42,43]. Thus, the drift in light intensity may be unavoidable in field analysis, due to the battery power consumption as well as the ambient temperature variation. On the other hand, to keep the basic communication function, the smartphone should be a detachable part in the portable detection device. Hence, it is also necessary to eliminate the influence from the variation in focus after reinstalling the smartphone or restarting the camera.

Herein, we report a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device for portable field analysis. By using a twin cuvette with sample and reference compartments, the fluorescent images of the luminophore solutions were captured by the built-in camera simultaneously. The brightness ratio between the compartments was calculated by a user-edited APP software and used as the analytical signal. The smartphone-based fluorescent device was applied in field analysis of soluble copper in river water samples, which is based on the fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [44–46]. Recently, various CQDs have emerged and attracted growing interesting in analytical and bioanalytical chemistry [47–51], due to the merits, such as excellent photostability, high quantum yield, good water solubility and biocompatibility. Furthermore, they can be prepared at large scale using cost-effective and eco-friendly methods [52–54]. By using N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) as the model luminophore, the analytical performance of this device was tested. The influence of the variations in light source intensity, ambient temperature, focus and shooting parameters of the camera, on the analytical performance was discussed. A balanced calibration method was used to improve the accuracy in concentration estimation. The smartphone-based ratiometric methods offer the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without purification. Ammonium citrate was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with specific resistance > 18 MΩ cm, which was obtained through a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA). The fluorescence and FTIR spectra were measured in spectrofluorimeter (F-7000, Hitachi) and FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR), respectively. The morphological evaluation was examined on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-800).

The smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device is illustrated in Fig. 1. A smartphone (Honor 7, China) was employed to capture the fluorescent image by its built-in camera as well as for data analysis. A quartz twin cuvette (5.5 ×12 ×40 mm for each compartment) and its frame were mounted in a black box with the dimensions of 120 × 80 × 60 mm. An LED flashlight (wavelength 365 nm, 3.7 V) was used as the excitation light source, powered by a portable lithium battery source. The light beam was perpendicular to the left wall of the cuvette. To compensate the exciting light intensity loss due to the shielding effect from the left compartment, a reflection film was placed behind the cuvette frame of right compartment. The reflection efficiency of film was adjusted to obtain identical fluorescence intensity between the sample and reference compartments (filled with 70 μg/mL N-CQDs). The camera was about 10 mm apart from the outside wall of the cuvette, with a long pass filter (cut wavelength < 420 nm) and a macro lens in the light path to get a clearer fluorescent image. Except the smartphone, the total cost of the device itself is about 120 $. To set the given shooting parameters, the auto-setting function of the camera was turned off. The exposure time (t) and photosensibility (ISO) of the camera were set artificially also via the APP software, which can be used in other smartphones in Android system. The averaged brightness of the blue component in fluorescent image in sample (
B
¯
S
) and reference (
B
¯
R
) areas is also measured by the user-edited APP software. The corrected brightness parameter (H) as well as the corrected brightness ratio (R) between two compartments were calculated by the method discussed in Section 3.1.

N-CQDs were prepared by a pyrolytic process according to ref. [55] with slight modifications. Briefly, 5 g of ammonium citrate was heated at 150 °C for 4 h, resulting in a brownish black powder of N-CQDs. The N-CQDs powder was ultrasoniced in ultrapure water for 1 h then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane (Millipore). The N-CQDs dispersion was stored at 4◦C for the further use. The concentration of N-CQDs dispersion was determined by a weight method.

As shown in Fig. S1 in Electronic Supporting Information (ESI), the diameters of the resultant N-CQDs are ranged from 3 to 5 nm. In FT-IR absorption spectrum (Fig. S2 in ESI), the absorption bands at 3440–3200, 1665 and 1506 cm−1 are corresponded to the O–H and N-H stretching vibration, C=O stretching and C–N bond stretching, respectively, indicating that the N-CQDs have plentiful hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface [56]. As a result, N-CQDs exhibit good dispersion stability in aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field analysis of Cu2+ in river water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The water samples were collected from Xiaoqing River (Jinan, China) and detected at the scene. Prior to the detection, the water samples were filtered through 0.45 µm membrane (Millipore). Then 1.00 mL of the filtered water sample was added to 5 mL plastic centrifuge tube and mixed with 3.00 mL phosphate buffer (pH=6.0, 2.4 mM Na2HPO4 + 17.6 mM NaH2PO4) containing 0.2 mM (NaPO3)6. 1.00 mL N-CQDs (350 μg/mL) was added and mixed thoroughly. Afterwards, 1.50 mL of the resultant mixture was added in the sample compartment (right). The initial brightness ratio was measured with the mixture containing 30 nM Cu2+ in the reference compartment (left). The initial concentration of Cu2+ (C x0) in water sample was estimated according to the stored calibration curve. If needed to improve the accuracy in the concentration estimation, a balanced calibration method was employed. The schematic block diagram of the APP software for field analysis in the balanced calibration method is shown in Fig. S3 in ESI. Three standard solutions containing 70 μg/mL N-CQDs and Cu2+ (in concentration of C 1 ≈0.8C x0, C 2 ≈C x0,C 3 ≈1.2C x0) were prepared and used as the references. After adjusting
B
¯
S
= 150 ± 20 via exposure time or ISO setting, the corrected brightness ratios were measured again using the new references. According to the data of (C 1, R 1), (C 2, R 2) and (C 3, R 3), a regression relation of C = a+bR was obtained. The concentration of Cu2+ in the water sample was calculated by C x = a+b (R=1). Where a and b are the linear fitting parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the smartphone based fluorescence device
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, the determination of soluble copper is based on the quenching effect of Cu2+ to the fluorescence of N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S4 in ESI, the excitation peak of N-CQDs aqueous dispersion is 348 nm. Based on the considerations for portability in field analysis, the proposed device did not equip both of the excitation and emission monochromers, which are the essential parts in a normal lab spectrofluorimeter. As an alternative, a commercially strong LED flashlight with the emission wavelength at 365 nm was chosen as the excitation source, which is close to excitation peak of N-CQDs. To eliminate the light scattering from the excitation source, a long pass filters was equipped. Under the conditions used, a blue fluorescent image was shown in the screen of the smartphone. The averaged brightness in the blue components (
B
̅
, 420 −510 nm) in the RGB model was measured and recorded as the fluorescent intensity indicator by the user-edited APP software.

Fig. 2 shows the correlation between the value of
B
̅
and fluorescence intensity (F) measured by an F-7000 spectrofluorimeter. It can be seen the value of
B
̅
is deviated from linearity at higher fluorescence intensity (F > 3000), which is due to the image brightness saturation phenomenon. Because the maximum brightness is limited to 255 by the image analysis software, the linear range of the smartphone-based detection device is obviously narrower than that of the lab spectrofluorimeter.

Under the experimental conditions used, the correlation between
B
̅
and F can be well fitted by following regressed model, which is similar to the Langmuir isotherm equation with saturation adsorption.
(1)
B
¯
=
B
0
¯
+
( 255 −
B
0
¯
) k F
1 + k F
Where
B
0
¯
is the averaged background brightness, k is a constant related to exposure time and ISO of the charge coupled device (CCD) in camera and photomultiplier tube (PMT) in the spectrofluorimeter.

In the case of kF<< 1, there is
B ¯
≈
B 0
¯
+ ( 255 −
B 0
¯
) k F
. Hence, the brightness of the fluorescent image is increased near-linearly with increasing fluorescent intensity in low concentration of N-CQDs. With further increasing F value in higher concentration of N-CQDs, the linear region is narrowed down due to increasing value of 1 +kF in the denominator in Eq. (1). The value of
B
̅
is approached to the maximum of 255 in the case of kF > >1.

Rearranging Eq. (1), we have
(2)
B
¯
−
B
0
¯
= ( 255 −
B
¯
) k F

According to Eq. (2), a corrected brightness parameter, H= kF, is obtained:
(3)
H = k F =
B
¯
−
B
0
¯
255 −
B
¯

As shown in Fig. 2, the curve of
(
B
¯
−
B
0
¯
) / ( 255 −
B
¯
)
versus F exhibits acceptable linearity with the correlation coefficient of r 2 = 0.9958, implying that the corrected brightness parameter is a better signal indicator than
B
̅
for fluorescence intensity measurement in the smartphone-based fluorescent device. It should be noted that such linear transformation is valid in the case with
B
̅
< 220, because the measurement error in H is enlarged observably when
B
̅
is approached to 255. On the other hand, the linearization is just based on the regression relation between
B
̅
and F in Eq. (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the shooting parameters on the performance of the smartphone based fluorescence device
ce:section.ce:para:
In the smartphone-based fluorescence device, the image brightness depends on the exposure time of the camera and ISO of the CCD. As shown in Fig. S5A in ESI, the brightness of the fluorescent image is increased non-linearly with increasing concentration of the N-CQDs, especially using longer exposure time (curve c, t = 8 s). One reason is that the exposure time used is much longer than the optimal range in the camera (usually 8–20 ms), narrowed markedly the dynamic range of the CCD. On the other hand, we employed a low quality CCD equipped in a cheap smartphone for fluorescent intensity measurement in this work. It is well known that the dynamic range of a CCD depends greatly on its quality [57,58]. To coordinate the contradiction between the sensitivity and linear range of the response curve, the total exposure time was divided to a group of shorter one with the accumulated intensity as the signal [59]. In this work, the corrected brightness parameter in Eq. (3) is used as signal indicator. As shown in Fig. S5B in ESI, the value of
(
B
¯
−
B
0
¯
) / ( 255 −
B
¯
)
is increased near-linearly with increasing concentration of N-CQDs. Under the experimental conditions used, the slopes of the response curves are 7.69, 28.1 and 73.6 L/g at the exposure time of 0.5, 2 and 8 s, respectively. Hence, the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) is enhanced with prolonging exposure time because the accumulated light signal is measured [60,61].

The ISO is a parameter related to the amplification factor in photoelectric conversion in the CCD. As shown in Fig. S6A in ESI, the correlation between
B
¯
S
and ISO is also non-linear. As a result, the brightness ratio is changed slightly with ISO in the case of unbalanced initial brightness in the double channels (Fig. S6B in ESI). For example, with ISO increased from 100 to 1600, the value of
B
¯
S
/
B
¯
R
is increased from 0.552 to 0.821 but decreased from 1.695 to 1.128 when the sample channel was filled with 20 and 80 μg/mL N-CQDs, respectively. The relative changes in
B
S
¯
/
B
R
¯
are still 49.2% and − 33.5%, although they are much less than those of 469% and 157% in
B
¯
S
itself. In addition, the
B
S
¯
/
B
R
¯
value is approached to 1 due to the brightness saturation at high ISO. Importantly, by using the corrected brightness parameter, the value of H S/H R is much insensitive to the change in ISO (Fig. S6C in ESI). In the ISO range from 100 to 1600, the values of H S/H R are 0.485 ± 0.008, 0.984 ± 0.009 and 2.14 ± 0.05 in sample channel filled with 20, 40 and 80 μg/mL N-CQDs, respectively. Consequently, the ISO value can be adjusted during the measurement process according to the initial brightness without measuring new calibration curve. It is even possible to exchange smartphone for ratiometric measurement if needed, especially in the case with similar brightness in the sample and reference channels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the focus of the camera on the response
ce:section.ce:para:
Different from a laboratory optical instrument with carefully fixed PMT position, the smartphone is a removable part in the proposed portable device for field detection. In capturing fluorescent image, the focus of the camera was readjusted manually after remounting the smartphone or restarting the camera, with the help of a ruler in transparent film. Obviously, it is necessary to maintain a constant focus for all the images in an analytical task. To test the repeatability of the proposed portable device, the smartphone was dismounted and remounted repeatedly. Filled with 40 μg/mL N-CQDs solution in the two channels, the relative standard deviations in ten independent measurements are 1.83% for
B
S
¯
/
B
R
¯
and 0.69% for H S/H R, respectively. Hence, the ratiometric strategy offers the convenience in focusing operation as the influence of focus on image is the same in the sample and reference areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of excitation source intensity drift on the responses
ce:section.ce:para: Because the fluorescent intensity is proportional to the intensity of the excitation light source, a high intensity excitation light source with excellent stability is desirable for fluorescence measurement. But in this work, a cheap LED UV flashlight was used as the excitation source. Compared with a normal laboratory spectrofluorimeter, the stability of the fluorescent signal in the simple device is expected to be at a disadvantage due to the limits in component quality and operation conditions in field analysis. On the other hand, the intensity of the flashlight is related to its supply voltage and ambient temperature.

To test the influence of intensity drift of the excitation, the supply voltage of the flashlight was adjusted artificially using a DC regulated power supply. As shown in Fig. S7A in ESI, the value of
B
S
¯
is increased near-linearly with increasing supplying voltage, due to the higher excitation intensity. The relative change in
B
S
¯
is related to the concentration of N-CQDs. For example, with the voltage increasing from 3.6 to 3.8 V, the values of
B
S
¯
in 20, 40, and 80 μg/mL N-CQDs solution are increased by 33.5%, 25.2% and 18.8%, respectively. However, the influence of intensity drift of the excitation source on the brightness ratio is much suppressed (Fig. S7B in ESI), especially in the case with similar initial brightness. When 40 μg/mL N-CQDs was added in the sample and reference cells, the values of
B
¯
S
/
B
¯
R
are 0.989 ± 0.006, indicating that the brightness ratio is independent of the variation in excitation intensity. But due to the non-linear correlation between the brightness and fluorescent intensity, the suppressing efficiency of the ratiometric strategy is reduced with enlarging brightness difference between the sample and reference channels. Under the experimental conditions used, the relative changes of
B
S
¯
/
B
R
¯
in 20 and 80 μg/mL N-CQDs solution are 6.6% and − 6.7%, respectively. Similarly, by using H S/H R as the indicator, the suppressing efficiency is improved obviously in case with unbalanced brightness between the double channels (Fig. S7C in ESI). With the voltage varied in the range of 3.7 ± 0.1 V, the values of H S/H R are 0.479 ± 0.003, 0.986 ± 0.003 and 2.18 ± 0.03 in sample channel with 20, 40, and 80 μg/mL N-CQDs solution, respectively. This result reveals again that H S/H R is a better indicator than
B
S
¯
/
B
R
¯
for the ratiometric strategy in the proposed smartphone-based fluorescent device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of ambient temperature on brightness ratio
ce:section.ce:para:
For a simple field analytical device, the ambient temperature is varied with time and location. The temperature dependent performance of the proposed device was tested in air-conditioned room temperature. As shown in Fig. S8A in ESI, the fluorescent intensity of N-CQDs is decreased with elevating temperature as the quantum yield of a luminophore is temperature dependent. It is reasonable to expect that the brightness ratio is insensitivity to the variation in solution temperature as the ambient temperature in the sample and reference cells is very close or the same, which is supported in the results in Fig. S8B and C in ESI. In the temperature range from 23° to 35°C, the averaged temperature coefficients are − 0.52% and 0.47%/°C using
B
S
¯
/
B
R
¯
, 0.11% and 0.06%/°C using H S/H R, in sample channel with 20 and 80 μg/mL N-CQDs solution, respectively. The low temperature coefficient in H S/H R is helpful to apply the proposed device in field analysis with varied ambient temperature surroundings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization for Cu2+ ion determination
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S9 in ESI, the fluorescent intensity of N-CQDs is reduced after adding of Cu2+. The fluorescent quenching process is rapid, depending mainly on the adsorption rate of Cu2+ to the surface of N-CQDs. The quenching mechanism of Cu2+ to the fluorescent of N-CQDs is due to interaction between their energy levels [44,45]. Under the mixing conditions used, the fluorescent intensity of N-CQDs approached to new stable level after 20 s. The short response time and long stable period are suited for field analysis. In fact, the time in solution mixing and cell washing is enough to obtain the stable fluorescent signal. On the other hand, the fluorescent intensity of N-CQDs is decreased with increasing concentration of the Cu2+, which is the base for Cu2+ determination. To enhance the sensitivity for Cu2+ determination, the experimental conditions, including pH and concentration of N-CQDs were optimized.

As shown in Fig. 3, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs exhibits the maximum at pH 4. After adding of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was decreased and the value of
B
R
¯
/
B
S
¯
researched the maximum at pH 6. Hence, the detection of Cu2+ were performed in the phosphate buffer (pH=6.0) in the following experiments. The influence of the concentration of N-CQDs on the fluorescence intensity is depicted in Fig. 4. With increasing N-CQDs concentration ranged from 10 to 120 μg/mL, both the values of
B
S
¯
and
B
R
¯
are increased. The maximum of
B
R
¯
/
B
S
¯
was obtained in 70 μg/mL N-CQDs solution. Hence, 70 μg/mL N-CQDs solution was chosen for Cu2+ determination.

Under the optimized conditions, the dependence of the fluorescence brightness and corrected brightness ratios on the concentration of Cu2+ is depicted in Fig. 5. It can be seen that the brightness of N-CQDs is reduced with increasing concentration of Cu2+. But the brightness response curve is in a non-linear shape, especially at higher concentration region (> 30 nM). It should be noted that the response in low concentration region is more important for trace analysis. Because the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs is less sensitive to eye, the pixel brightness of an image is calculated by: L= 0.30L R+ 0.59L G + 0.11L B, where L R, L G and L B are the brightness in red, green and blue components in RGB mode, respectively. As a result, the fluorescent brightness of N-CQDs in the presence of 0.2 μM Cu2+ is weak in visual sense although
B
S
¯
= 39.8 (Fig. 5A)

By using the corrected brightness ratio of the reference to sample areas, the value of H R /H S is in a near-linear dependent on the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0.003–0.2 μM. The limit of detection (LoD) of the proposed method for Cu2+ is estimated by:
(4)
LoD =
3
σ
0
C
1
H
R
/
H
S1
−
H
R
/
H
S0
Where σ0 is the standard deviation of the H S0/H R in blank solution, H R/H S1 is the corrected brightness ratio in a low concentration of C 1 (5 nM), respectively.

Under the experimental conditions used, the LoD for Cu2+ is 0.43 nM, which is much lower than the maximum contaminant level of 20 μM for copper in drinking water according to U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [11]. Table 1 lists the analytical performance of some methods reporting the determination of Cu2+, indicating that the proposed method offers an acceptable sensitivity for practical applications without enrichment treatment.

To perform the field analysis reliably, we test the selectivity of the proposed method. As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescence of N-CQDs is hardly influenced by these common ions in clear water sample except Fe3+. The slight interfere from Fe3+ can be reduced by using sodium hexametaphoshpate as a chelating agent for Fe3+. In the presence of these common ions, the fluorescence quench degrees of N-CQDs by Cu2+ are similar. Thus, the present fluorescent method possesses an acceptable selectivity for Cu2+ detection. It should be noted that CQDs prepared from different starting materials or by different methods show different sensitivity [62]. For example, Zhao and co-workers reported a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of mercury ions using CQDs prepared from solvothermal treating corn bract [63]. Liao et. al prepared phosphorus, nitrogen co-doped CQDs (P,N-CQDs) via hydrothermal protocol employing pyridoxal 5-phosphate and ethanediamine [64]. The resultant P,N-CQDs were used as a highly selective fluorescent probe for cobalt ion. They also reported as highly selective fluorescent probe for silver ion detection using sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CQDs) synthesized from citric acid and thiamine hydrochloride via a one-step hydrothermal protocol [65]. In the work of Zhou and co-workers [66], the CQDs synthesized via a hydrothermal method using citric acid and Tris as precursors exhibited better fluorescent response toward Fe(III) than Cu(II) and was applied in bioimaging of Fe(III) in living cells. Ma et. al reported a ratiometric fluorescent method for the determination of Cr(VI) using dual emissive carbon dots synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of m-aminophenol and oxalic acid [67]. He and co-workers reported a selective fluorescent nanoprobe for Fe3+ detection in biological samples based on ultrabright N/P codoped carbon dots [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in field analysis of Cu2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The low baseline drift in ratiometric strategy is helpful in field analysis. To demonstrate the applicability of the proposed device, we performed the field analysis of Cu2+ in real water samples at five sites in Xiaoqing River (Jinan, Shandong province). Based on the consideration of environmental protection, the normal digestion processes to decompose the samples by concentrated HNO3 + H2SO4 and evaporated to dryness were not employed. On the other hand, the UV digestion technique was not used due to the limit of power supply in field analysis. Thus, part of copper in colloid particles or complexes is not detected in the proposed method without digestion treatment. The concentrations of Cu(II) in river water samples determined by the proposed method is considered as the soluble copper. As listed in Table 2, the concentrations of Cu2+ determined by the smartphone-based portable device are in good agreement with those obtained in the laboratory using a spectrofluorimeter. In addition, the recoveries for Cu2+ in the filtered water samples are in the range of 96–106% (Table S1 in ESI), indicating acceptable accuracy for the field analysis. But the concentrations of Cu2+ detected by the fluorescence method are systematically lower than those obtained by ICP-AES method, because the total amount in the water samples were detected in latter. To address such difference, the unfiltered water samples were digested in laboratory by the method described in ESI. After digestion treatment, the concentrations of Cu2+ determined by the fluorescent method are quite in accord with those provided by the ICP-AES method (Table 2). On the other hand, the variation tendencies in the concentrations of soluble and total copper in river water samples are similar. Hence, the proposed field analysis method can be used also in rapid sample screening for total copper determination in rivers.

In summary, we developed a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent device for field analysis. By using the corrected brightness ratio between the double channels in a twin cuvette, the baseline drift due to the variation in excitation light intensity, shooting parameters and focus of the camera, and ambient temperature is suppressed significantly, which is helpful to improve the stability and reliability of the simple portable detection device. The balanced calibration method can improve the accuracy for concentration determination due to favorable ratiometric efficiency. This cheap fluorescence device offers the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575080 and 21874083) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2018MB029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.10.019.
